pattern: A wildcard move can be resolved in whichever direction is more useful, so the furthest reachable point is just the net displacement from the fixed moves plus every wildcard pushing the same way -- no simulation of individual paths is needed, only three counts.
hints: Separate the string into three counts: how many forced left moves, how many forced right moves, and how many wildcard moves that could go either way.

The fixed moves already give you a net displacement (right count minus left count); every wildcard move should be sent in whichever direction increases the distance from the origin, and since they're interchangeable, sending all of them the same way is always at least as good as splitting them.

The furthest distance is the absolute value of (right count minus left count) plus the wildcard count -- committing every wildcard to reinforce the existing net direction (or to build one from scratch if the fixed moves cancel out) always maximizes the final distance from 0.

edge_inputs.description: no wildcards at all, pure forced moves
forced moves exactly cancel out, only wildcards contribute

stress.description: random mixes of L, R, and wildcard characters at lengths up to the cap
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 50))
    s = ''.join(rng.choice('LR_') for _ in range(n))
    return [s]
